family_audit: Treat round 35 as the completed recovery step. The restored surface re-measured at 24.175471 ms, which is close enough to the accepted 24.164272 ms base that round 36 should spend budget on a new structural family rather than on another restore.
Keep the exact pair-scratch PTX export batching sub-variant closed. Round 34 already showed that it preserved correctness but failed to improve the end-to-end result.
Promote the active PTX one-K 128x128 control-path exploit to rank 1 again because the live source is back on the accepted PTX anchor and still shows the same low-warps / long-scoreboard plateau.
Keep the 256x128 low-register transplant as the highest-ceiling alternative even though it remains more correctness-sensitive than the PTX-local follow-ups.
Keep one narrower steady-state barrier / handoff retime in the set, but make it explicit that this is a different scheduler-seam probe from the already-closed export-scratch batching attempt.

directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: The current source is back on the accepted PTX anchor, and the recovery measurement reconfirms the same structural signature: the hot-band PTX kernel still dominates runtime at roughly the same end-to-end cost, active warps remain pinned near 16.62%, registers stay occupancy-limited at 2 CTAs, and long-scoreboard remains elevated at 7.17%. Round 34 ruled out one specific export-scratch trimming idea, but it did not invalidate the whole active-PTX family. The best next move is therefore a bounded control-path exploit on the restored one-K PTX branch that avoids replaying the failed pair-scratch export batching and instead targets remaining consume-order, export-address, or live-range friction on the hot path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and live-range pressure on the accepted one-K 128x128 branch.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays on the winning family and acts on the live hot path, but PTX control-path edits are still correctness-sensitive and can easily reshuffle stalls without creating a real runtime win.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus 24.164272 ms and 24.175471 ms
ranking_notes: This is the highest-priority open family that is still aligned with the restored PTX winner.
It ranks above the 256x128 family because the implementation surface is smaller and the live metrics still point at the PTX control path as the most defensible next exploit.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit collapses into a replay of the already-closed pair-scratch export batching idea or if it leaves both runtime and the long-scoreboard signature unchanged.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 family remains the only route in repo history that ever hinted at low-30% active warps, which is why it stays ahead of any smaller cleanup family despite its risk. The restored PTX anchor proves that the loop is back on a clean comparison surface, so if a high-ceiling geometry jump is going to be attempted again, this is still the one worth keeping live. The guardrail is the same as before: transplant the low-register staging and reuse ideas into the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot without reviving the broken half-panel writer split.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and synchronization strategy on the 256x128 hot-band path, plus correctness-sensitive writer ownership.
directions.risk: High. This is still the broadest and most correctness-sensitive active family.
ranking_notes: Keep this family second because it still offers the highest visible occupancy ceiling in repo history.
It stays below dir_01 because the current restored PTX surface is already strong enough that a smaller live-branch exploit deserves one more chance before another broad correctness-risky jump.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on correctness failure or on another 28-30 ms class regression without a decisive occupancy breakthrough.

directions.name: Steady-state Barrier / Handoff Retime
directions.hypothesis: The restored PTX anchor is back on the live hot path, which makes the old scheduler-seam family actionable again. This is deliberately narrower and different from the failed round-34 export batching change: instead of adding extra export scratch and pairing stores, the goal here is to retime the steady-state seam around `cp_async_wait_group_0`, `__syncthreads()`, and the future-tile refill order inside the PTX hot-band loop. The current counters still leave room for that kind of seam-level retime because barrier remains around 5.42% and long-scoreboard around 7.17% even on the restored anchor.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual wait-group and barrier cadence in the active PTX hot-band steady-state loop, especially the handoff between MMA issue completion and future-tile refill.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family is bounded, but scheduler retimes can easily swap barrier and scoreboard pressure without producing a measurable end-to-end gain.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164272 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Keep one scheduler-seam family in the set because the restored PTX microkernel is again the dominant hot path.
It ranks below dir_02 because the exact export-batching sub-variant already failed and the remaining handoff retime upside is likely narrower.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation drifts back into export-scratch growth or if barrier and scoreboard simply trade places without a runtime improvement.

notes: Round 36 should not waste another slot on restore. The loop is already back on a clean PTX anchor, and the latest measurement at 24.175471 ms is close enough to the accepted 24.164272 ms baseline to resume real exploration. The ranking therefore moves back to live, unabsorbed families on the accepted surface: a bounded PTX control-path exploit first, the broader 256x128 low-register branch second, and a narrower steady-state handoff retime third.
